_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Gloucester County, VA. Record survival for a Middle Peninsula county — records survive from the 1650s but with some Civil War-era losses.

current_courthouse.location: Gloucester, VA — 7400 Justice Drive (modern judicial center)

previous_courthouses.location: Gloucester Court House village — early structures at the crossroads
Gloucester Court House — colonial-era brick courthouse, one of the oldest surviving courthouses in Virginia
previous_courthouses.fate: The 1766 courthouse survived the Revolution and Civil War. It stands today as a historic structure, though court functions moved to the modern building.

record_losses.cause: Civil War — military operations near Gloucester Point
record_losses.description: Gloucester County's courthouse village was behind the primary battle lines during both the Revolution and the Civil War. The 1766 courthouse survived intact. Some loose papers were lost during the Civil War when Union forces controlled Gloucester Point, but the bound record volumes survived.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Gloucester's records survive from 1651 with relatively minor losses. The 1766 courthouse — still standing — held records that span the colonial, revolutionary, and antebellum periods. {ancestor_name}'s paper trail in Gloucester has a good chance of survival.
record_losses.source: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Good — deed books survive from 1651 with some gaps in the earliest volumes.
deeds.gaps: Some early deed books have water damage. Generally continuous from the mid-17th century.
deeds.repository: Gloucester County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia, Richmond
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections; Ancestry.com VA land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — will books from 1651. Estate inventories are particularly detailed for the colonial period.
wills_estates.gaps: Some loose estate papers from early periods are incomplete.
wills_estates.repository: Gloucester County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Marriage bonds from 1780. Earlier marriages in Abingdon Parish records and court order entries.
marriages.gaps: No bonds before 1780. Church records partially fill the gap.
marriages.repository: Gloucester County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

court_minutes.completeness: Good — order books from 1651 with some gaps.
court_minutes.gaps: Some gaps in the earliest volumes. More continuous from the late 17th century onward.
court_minutes.repository: Library of Virginia; Gloucester County Clerk of Court

vital_records.completeness: Virginia state registration began 1912. Earlier vital events in church records.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records

church_records.parish: Abingdon Parish (Church of England / Episcopal) and Petsworth Parish
church_records.completeness: Abingdon Parish vestry book survives from 1677 — an important colonial record. Petsworth Parish vestry book also survives with processioning records and tithable lists.
church_records.repository: Library of Virginia; Virginia Historical Society; published transcriptions

substitute_sources.description: Gloucester County was served by two colonial parishes — Abingdon and Petsworth. Both vestry books survive, providing processioning records, tithable lists, poor relief, and vestry minutes that supplement the civil record.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Virginia; published transcriptions by Chamberlayne
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Parish processioning records describe property boundaries with reference to neighbors — invaluable for placing families in the landscape. Tithable lists function as proto-census records.
substitute_sources.source: Chamberlayne, C. G., ed. The Vestry Book and Register of Abingdon Parish, Gloucester County, Virginia. Richmond, 1912.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Agricultural and slave schedules (1850–1860) are particularly important for understanding the dual economy (farming and waterman communities).
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Census records track the distinctive waterman families who may not appear in land records because they owned boats rather than farms.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Gloucester County, VA.

substitute_sources.description: Land patents from the Crown survive from 1623 at the Library of Virginia. These document early land grants in Gloucester County, formed in 1651.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Virginia; published abstracts by Nugent
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Patent books document the earliest land grants and headright claims in Gloucester County.
substitute_sources.source: Nugent, Nell Marion. Cavaliers and Pioneers. Richmond: Virginia State Library, 1934.

narrative_hooks: Gloucester's 1766 courthouse still stands — one of the oldest surviving courthouses in Virginia — and the records inside it go back to 1651, when the county was formed from York County as English settlement pushed across the York River.

If {ancestor_name} was a waterman rather than a farmer, {pronoun_subject} may not appear in the deed books at all — watermen owned boats, not land, and the county record captures the planter class more completely than the creek-bank families who made their living from the water.

Abingdon Parish's vestry records describe the boundaries of {ancestor_name}'s property by reference to {pronoun_possessive} neighbors' land — the processioning records are a landscape survey that places families in physical space, not just legal record.
The 1766 courthouse at Gloucester Court House survived the Revolution and the Civil War — {ancestor_name}'s deed from {year} sat in that vault through both conflicts and is likely there still.
source_references: Chamberlayne, C. G., ed. The Vestry Book and Register of Abingdon Parish, Gloucester County, Virginia. Richmond, 1912.
Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.
Nugent, Nell Marion. Cavaliers and Pioneers. Richmond: Virginia State Library, 1934.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Gloucester County, VA.
